title: Dog Frequent Squatting with Drops of Urine: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog squatting often with only drops of urine? Learn about UTIs, bladder stones, and when you need to see an emergency vet.
content_html:
What is Frequent Squatting and Dribbling?

When a dog squats repeatedly but only produces small drops of urine, it is known as pollakiuria (increased frequency) and stranguria (straining). This behavior typically indicates that the bladder lining or urethra is highly irritated. Common causes include bacterial urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, crystals in the urine, or inflammation of the bladder wall.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is Medium. If your dog is still passing at least some urine, you should schedule a veterinary appointment as soon as possible, ideally within 24 hours, as these conditions are painful. However, if your dog is straining and nothing at all is coming out, this is a High Urgency medical emergency known as a urinary blockage, which can be fatal if not treated immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a photo or video can significantly speed up the diagnosis. A video of the squatting behavior helps the vet distinguish between behavioral marking and physical straining. Additionally, a photo of the urine drops on a light surface can reveal if there is blood (hematuria) or cloudiness, which helps determine the severity of the inflammation before your dog even enters the exam room.

faqs.question: Could this just be my dog marking their territory?
faqs.answer: Territory marking is usually a controlled behavior. If your dog seems restless, displays a tensed posture, or tries to urinate every few steps, it is more likely a medical issue than marking.

faqs.question: Is it okay to wait a few days to see if it resolves?
faqs.answer: No. Urinary issues are uncomfortable and can quickly escalate into kidney infections or life-threatening blockages. Prompt veterinary care is necessary for the dog's comfort and safety.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to help?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam and a urinalysis to check for bacteria, blood, and crystals. They may also recommend an X-ray or ultrasound to look for bladder stones.
